lessons.preparation: - Print copies of the Unit Guide (one for each student)

vocabularies.definition: the practice of protecting computers, networks, and personal data from being stolen, changed, or damaged

objectives.description: Describe how failures may stem from poor design, user error, or technical breakdowns
Identify ways digital systems can fail and impact users’ daily lives
Reflect on how failures affect different users emotionally and functionally

standards.description: Evaluate the ways computing impacts personal, ethical, social, economic, and cultural practices.
Explain security issues that might lead to compromised computer programs.
Evaluate computational artifacts to maximize their beneficial effects and minimize harmful effects on society.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Introduction to Cybersecurity

lessons.preparation: - Print copies of the Cybersecurity Threats activity guide (one for each student)

vocabularies.definition: software intended to damage a computing system or to take partial control over its operation
any potential cause of harm to a computer system or network
a weakness in a system that could be exploited by an attacker

objectives.description: Describe system-level threats such as malware, ransomware, weak default settings, or misconfigured file sharing.
Explain how AI may increase the speed or scale of these threats.
Explain how attackers exploit technical vulnerabilities to gain control, disrupt access, or corrupt data.

standards.description: Evaluate the ways computing impacts personal, ethical, social, economic, and cultural practices.
Give examples to illustrate how sensitive data can be affected by malware and other attacks.
Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Describe how artificial intelligence drives many software and physical systems.
Explain security issues that might lead to compromised computer programs.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Cybersecurity Threats

lessons.preparation: - Provide copies of the Hackable Human activity guide (one for each student)

vocabularies.definition: a technique that attempts to trick a user into providing personal information. That personal information can then be used to access sensitive online resources, such as bank accounts and emails
a malicious activity aimed at disrupting, damaging, destroying or gaining unauthorized access to information systems, networks or the data they contain

objectives.description: Analyze how phishing and password attacks exploit human behavior— such as trust, emotion, and predictability
Investigate identity-based cyber threats by analyzing phishing messages and password-related breaches

standards.description: Evaluate the social and economic implications of privacy in the context of safety, law, or ethics.
Give examples to illustrate how sensitive data can be affected by malware and other attacks.
Explain security issues that might lead to compromised computer programs.
Compare ways software developers protect devices and information from unauthorized access.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Phishing and Password Attacks

lessons.preparation: - Print copies of the Phishing Prevention Strategies Activity Guide (one for each student)

vocabularies.definition: a video or audio that has been manipulated using AI to look or sound like a real person
a type of phishing that happens over text message (SMS)
manipulating people into giving up private information or doing something risky
a type of phishing that happens over phone call or voicemail

objectives.description: Compare basic strategies for prevention
Examine how attackers use deception to manipulate people into revealing sensitive information or taking risky actions
Explain how AI tools (e.g., generative text, deepfake video, voice cloning, chatbot mimicry) increase the realism, reach, and personalization of social engineering attacks

standards.description: Give examples to illustrate how sensitive data can be affected by malware and other attacks.
Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Describe how artificial intelligence drives many software and physical systems.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Phishing with AI

lessons.preparation:
- Print copies of the Defense in Action activity guide (one per student)
- Print copies of the Case Studies (one case study per student)

objectives.description: Evaluate different defenses to determine what made them effective
Explain how system design, human behavior, and AI can work together to prevent a cyber threat

standards.description: Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Describe how artificial intelligence drives many software and physical systems.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Successful Cybersecurity Stories

lessons.preparation:
- Prepare copies of the Investigate a CIA Scenario: Cases handout (one per group)
- Prepare copies of the Investigate a CIA Scenario: Attacker/Defender activity guide (one per group)

Student.name: Investigate a CIA Scenario - Scenario Files

vocabularies.definition: a set of core rules for protecting information and stands for Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability
making information accessible to the people who need it, when they need it
keeping information private so only the right people can see it
ensuring that information stays correct and hasn't been changed without permission

objectives.description: Describe how a system vulnerability allowed a confidentiality, integrity, or availability failure and propose a user-centered fix
Identify cybersecurity failures based on their impact on confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA Triad)

standards.description: Give examples to illustrate how sensitive data can be affected by malware and other attacks.
Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Compare various security measures, considering tradeoffs between the usability and security of a computer system.
Explain security issues that might lead to compromised computer programs.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability

lessons.preparation:
- Print and cut out one full set of Access Control Scenario Cards for the entire class
- Print and cut out a set of Authentication Method Cards (one copy per station)
- Print out the Station Instruction Pages (pages 3-6 in the Station Setup and Instructions teacher resource)
- Provide multiple sticky notes for each station

vocabularies.definition: a set of tools used in cybersecurity to help systems protect information and manage what users can do
when a system keeps track of what users do so suspicious activity can be detected and reviewed
the process a system uses to check that you are who you say you are
rules a system uses to decide what you’re allowed to do once you’re logged in

objectives.description: Apply authentication and access control models to real-world scenarios
Explain how systems verify identity and manage access to information

standards.description: Explain how abstractions hide the underlying implementation details of computing systems embedded in everyday objects.
Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Compare various security measures, considering tradeoffs between the usability and security of a computer system.
Compare ways software developers protect devices and information from unauthorized access.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting

lessons.preparation:
- Print copies of the Fraud Detection Investigation Activity Guide (one copy per group)
- Print copies of the four different user profiles

Teacher.name: [KEY] User Profiles' Audit Log Suspicious Activity

objectives.description: Analyze how AI supports security features like fraud detection, anomaly detection, and adaptive authentication
Explain how AI is used in threat detection to identify suspicious patterns

standards.description: Create computational models that represent the relationships among different elements of data collected from a phenomenon or process.
Describe how artificial intelligence drives many software and physical systems.
Use data analysis tools and techniques to identify patterns in data representing complex systems.

lessons.title: Lesson 8: AAA and AI

lessons.preparation:
- Print copies of the Encryption Design Challenge activity guide (one for each group of students)
- (Optional) Print copies of the Design Challenge Inspiration Cards
- (Optional) Print copies of the Caesar Cipher Wheel

vocabularies.definition: a process that reverses encryption, taking a secret message and reproducing the original plain text
a process of encoding messages to keep them secret, so only "authorized" parties can read it
encryption that uses two different keys: a public key to lock the message and a private key to unlock it
encryption where the same key is used to lock and unlock the message

objectives.description: Apply encryption concepts to different data states (at rest, in transit, in use)
Describe how encryption helps protect data
Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encryption

standards.description: Create prototypes that use algorithms to solve computational problems by leveraging prior student knowledge and personal interests.
Explain how abstractions hide the underlying implementation details of computing systems embedded in everyday objects.
Compare ways software developers protect devices and information from unauthorized access.

lessons.title: Lesson 9: Encryption

lessons.preparation: - Print copies of the AI in Encryption activity guide (one for each student)

objectives.description: Analyze a tradeoff introduced by AI’s role in encryption
Identify ways AI can both weaken and strengthen encryption
Model how AI and computational power influence the effectiveness of encryption systems

standards.description: Compare various security measures, considering tradeoffs between the usability and security of a computer system.
Describe how artificial intelligence drives many software and physical systems.
Use data analysis tools and techniques to identify patterns in data representing complex systems.
Evaluate computational artifacts to maximize their beneficial effects and minimize harmful effects on society.

lessons.title: Lesson 10: AI in Encryption

lessons.preparation:
- Print copies of the Redesign Challenge activity guide (one per group)
- Print copies of the Security vs Usability Matrix handout (one per group)

objectives.description: Describe how a security feature might unintentionally create a barrier for users
Redesign a security feature to better balance security and usability

standards.description: Evaluate and refine computational artifacts to make them more usable and accessible.
Test and refine computational artifacts to reduce bias and equity deficits.
Compare various security measures, considering tradeoffs between the usability and security of a computer system.

lessons.title: Lesson 11: Security vs Usability

lessons.preparation:
- Provide green, blue, and yellow colored pencils for students to use
- Print copies of the Layered Defenses Activity Guide (one for each student)

vocabularies.definition: using multiple, independent security features to protect systems and data

objectives.description: Apply computational thinking to analyze cyberattacks, identify system vulnerabilities, and reason through defense strategies
Design a layered defense that balances protection, usability, and constraints
Evaluate design choices in system features that balance security and usability, including those enhanced by AI

standards.description: Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Compare various security measures, considering tradeoffs between the usability and security of a computer system.
Explain tradeoffs when selecting and implementing cybersecurity recommendations.
Compare ways software developers protect devices and information from unauthorized access.

lessons.title: Lesson 12: Layered Defenses

lessons.preparation: - Print copies of the AI Powered Threats and Defenses Project Guide (one for each pair of students)

Student.name: Designing Secure Systems Project Planning Guide

objectives.description: Design secure system improvements that balance protection with usability, using trade-off reasoning
Explain how solutions work and how they help real users
Identify security and usability issues in a digital system by analyzing user needs and risks

standards.description: Create prototypes that use algorithms to solve computational problems by leveraging prior student knowledge and personal interests.
Evaluate and refine computational artifacts to make them more usable and accessible.
Document design decisions using text, graphics, presentations, and/or demonstrations in the development of complex programs.
Recommend security measures to address various scenarios based on factors such as efficiency, feasibility, and ethical impacts.
Compare various security measures, considering tradeoffs between the usability and security of a computer system.

lessons.title: Lesson 13: Designing Secure Systems Project
